speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2 of the interview with Richard Pick in Mexico City, Mexico. I'd like to ask you about your work at the Auswandererstelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our work, as I mentioned before, we were the intermediary between the German authorities and the potential immigrants. Unfortunately, if I say potential immigrants, many of those potential immigrants, they never made it. They never made it not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on account of a lack of trying. They tried very, very hard, all of us. But unfortunately at the time, there were very, very few countries, in fact, I must say there was none, maybe the United States. But they had that immigration quota, which was very limited. And most people just couldn't make it, unless you had financial means, money outside of Germany, which, at that time, was prohibited to have outside of Germany. You were supposed to bring everything you had outside of Germany, bring it back, which made it more difficult, too, I mean, to-- for transactions if you wanted to, to leave. But unless you had money outside of Germany or you had very good contacts in the foreign countries, it was very difficult to, to get out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nowadays, they're all big heroes, even including the United States. Pardon me for saying so, because I'm a US citizen. But at the time, it was just banging your head against the wall. And there were very few possibilities to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too late too, already, you know? It was in 1939, 1940. It became more difficult as time went by.  It became more and-- difficult and more difficult. And I myself, I didn't have any contacts whatever. My mother had a, a cousin once-removed in the United States. But a letter took-- a letter at the time took about six weeks, you know. So back and forth, by the time you were answered, it was a matter of, of almost three months. So it, it was very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could send telegrams with 10 words. But again, to send a telegram, you needed all kinds of authorizations and filing from the Gestapo, you know, to send a telegram.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. At this time, your family was in Czechoslovakia, and you were in Stuttgart with your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate? Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: By letter. We communicated by letter. But you, you were already afraid to write everything the way you felt because you were never sure whether letters were not opened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely, they were not at that time. But then in 1940-- don't forget, in 1940 and 1941-- Germany entered the war in 1939, September 1st, 1939, when, when they-- when they attacked Poland. Before, of course, there was the Anschluss of Austria. And there was the invasion in, in, in, Sudetenland, in Czechoslovakia. But since the Czech never put up any resistance, since they were let down by the-- by France and by England, even though they had a pact, a non-aggression pact. But when they were being attacked by the Germans, the, the Frenchmen didn't do anything, and the English didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then of course, you remember then Chamberlain came and the whole story. But this was a problem. Hitler could take one by one at that time, you know? Mm-hmm. How was your family surviving in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] my father sold what they had taken along. They had taken all the, the household goods along. We had eight rooms in, in, in Germany. I still have two chairs left upstairs, big wooden chairs. And my father sold-- it was-- some were very fine things. They had a, a big-- a big piano. What do you call them, the-- with the-- these-- concert pianos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grand piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my father, he started selling piece by piece. They lived off this. And then my father, he did some commission business there, but everything [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unofficially. I mean, that he couldn't go out and sell officially. So he, he brought a buyer and seller together of a house. And he got a little commission. But these things, unfortunately, didn't happen too often. So my parents, they just got by. They just got by in [NON-ENGLISH], you know. And then, when the Sudetenland was invaded by the Germans, then they went to Brunn, which is further inland. And then from Brunn, finally, in November, 1941, they got-- they got deported. Now, in the meantime, I-- in Germany, I tried to emigrate. But I didn't have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] much luck. And we were two-- we were three friends, Lothar Levine, and then there was Herbert Dreyfuss and myself. And we, we just didn't know how to go about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said, why don't we write to the-- all the consulates and the embassies? And we got a list of the consulates and the embassies. And we made a nice poem. Unfortunately, I don't have a copy of it anymore. And we sent it to all these embassies, which must have been at least-- and the consulates. All over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Within Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, within Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, don't forget, at that time-- we are talking about now 1940, '41. At that time, Germany had won the war already against France. France was occupied. We, in Germany, we were under English air raids. I went to the-- to the air-raid shelter with my grandmother many, many nights, sometimes two, three times a week. We were assigned an air-raid shelter. Now, this sounds paradox. We were assigned an air-raid shelter, in a-- not in, in the house where we lived, but in someone else's house. We had to walk about half a block-- run half a block to the air-raid shelter. And we were sitting there with the gentiles. In one corner, we were sitting, I mean, not because they told us we should sit in the corner, but we just felt that way. And there were-- there were bombs falling, not too many in Stuttgart at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sent that letter out. And we must have sent, I would say, between 40 and 60 letters. And what year was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- that must have been at the end of 1940, I would say. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got three or four responses. The rest of them, they didn't even answer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the three or four responses we got, we got-- three said they are very sorry. They can't do anything for us presently. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was one in Bremen, a letter which told us that if we would bring valid passports to Bremen that he might be able to do something. We should bring two photographs and birth certificates, et cetera, et cetera, which we did. Now, this was a, a black consul of Liberia. And he, he told us-- we had talked to him on the phone before. He told us, now, I can give you the visa for Liberia. But I cannot give you the landing permit. The landing permit is up to the Department of the Interior. And that you cannot get. Just forget about it. But I can give you a visa, yes. Don't even apply for a landing permit, he said, because you won't have a chance. But with that visa, maybe you can get a transit visa through Spain or Portugal to get out of Germany. So he gave us this visa in our passports. And we needed now a, a ship, you know, to-- I mean, officially to take us to Liberia. We knew we couldn't-- never go to Liberia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, the capital of Liberia is Monrovia. It's in Africa, West Africa, where that belly of Africa comes out, sticks out. That's where Liberia is. I didn't know it at the time, but I looked it up in the map. But we-- I remember we bought mosquito netting. We bought rubber boots because you couldn't get any leather boots. And we, we got malaria tablets, and-- even though we knew we probably would never to get to Liberia. But still, for us, it was Liberia, you know, in our minds.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Stuttgart, in the Auswandererstelle, we inquired. And we were told that we-- the only possibility to get out was on a freighter, on a freighter from, from Spain to Liberia. And they were coming there about once every three weeks or so. So we called up the Spanish Consulate in Munich. That was a Graf-- Backenheim was his name, Von Backenheim-- Count Von Backenheim. And we told him that we had-- he was an honorary consul.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German. But he was an honorary consul for Spain. At that time, we shouldn't forget that the Civil War in Spain was just over. And the Germans had helped, you know, the Franco to win that war against the Communists.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we contacted that consulate, the Spanish Consulate in, in Munich. And the secretary told us that we might be able to get a transit visa if we had that visa for Liberia. But we would have to bring our, our bookings for the-- for the ship-- for the freighter. So how could we get the bookings for the freighter? We didn't even have the money, you know? So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew a girl who-- through the business transactions, if I may call them so, with the immigrants. She worked at the Reiseburo, which is a tourist agency-- travel agency. Rominger was the name, in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went there for other people-- you know, other people who had their train tickets, so I picked them up. I told her, I said, I have-- we have now the, the visa for Liberia. And I want to get a transit visa. But we don't have the, the bookings, you know, for the-- for a ship. And then she looked it up. And she saw that-- the freighters and the plan. And she called up at that particular line. And she gave us the data that, that those-- that boat was leaving about every three or four weeks from Cadiz, which is at the southern tip of Spain, near Gibraltar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I said, I mean, how can I get? She said-- she was very nice. She said, well, listen, I don't think I can help you very much. But there is stationery in my-- in the drawer of my desk. And my boss just called me. I have to go into the other room.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took out-- I took out six or eight sheets of the Reiseburo Rominger with the letterhead. And I waited. I had the-- one of these [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] portfolios with me to put everything in. I had many other papers there. I put it in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she came back, I-- we still talked about other things. And I said, thank you very much. And we wrote our-- I wrote up that letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you remember what her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She has a very bad-sounding name in German. Her name was Fraulein Fick--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --F-I-C-K, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a Gentile. She was a nice-looking girl. She must have been-- at the time, she must have been six or eight years older than I was. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the paper. I wrote up the-- I, I knew all these type of letters and-- because I worked there, you know. I mean, I, I did these things all day long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wrote up a very nice letter that the-- that the-- we were booked-- I was booked on such and such a ship, leaving-- according to the itinerary which she had given me, leaving such and such a date, and that everything was paid for. Yes. And I just put it on there. So we made an appointment with the Spanish Consulate. I made the appointment for myself, because we were three, actually, yes? And we, we, we draw a lottery, you know, who was going first. And I was the first one to go to Munich. So when I came to Munich-- in Munich, usually it's raining. It was raining very hard. And I went to the-- to the consulate. I was there one night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, I went to the consulate. And I was sitting there. And I told the secretary what, what I wanted. And she said, well, just a moment. And that, that, that Graf Von Backenheim, he came out. He was limping. He had-- I think from the First World War, he had a, a lesion there. And he told me to come in and to show him my papers. I was shaking inside. I showed him my papers. He looked-- he looked at the permit. He looked at my passport. He looked at the, the letter of Rominger, the Reiseburo Rominger. And he said, just a moment. And he went to the next room. I said, that's the end. I figured he's going to call up that Reiseburo Rominger now. I, really-- I was-- I was always ready to run out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then he came back after about two or three minutes, which to me seemed to be an eternity. And he didn't say a word.  He opened his desk. And he took out a stamp. And he put that visa in my passport, a transit visa, which was good for two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I could actually-- according to the transit visa, I could leave Germany, if I could leave Germany, anytime. But whenever I came to Spain, it was good for two weeks. It had an open date, but only good for two weeks. So I came back to Germany, to Stuttgart. And then the other two friends went there, too, with my experiences. And they got it, too. One of the two never got out of Germany. He was killed. Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lothar Levine, he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Nazis, yes. Well, I don't-- I don't like that word 'Nazi.' For me, it's by the Germans. Nowadays they use 'Nazis' to distinguish between what was then-- and the good Germans and the bad Germans. To me, they were all bad Germans with very few, very few exceptions. They were all on the negative scale. Some were only minus 1 and some were minus 100. But they were all on the negative side. There were very few good Germans, very, very few. And I'll tell you a little story later on about the good Germans when we talk about Dr. Kurtz in the Seventh Army Interrogation Center.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, how did we get out? Again, I went to the Reiseburo Rominger. I went to the girl. And she said, listen. There is no-- there's nothing written that a, a Jew, if he has the permits, that he couldn't go out by plane. But of course, at the time, there was no plane. It was all military, understand? Germany was at war. There were-- there were-- there were announcements in the daily papers, you know, of German soldiers, of officers that had given their, their lives for the Fuhrer and for the Fatherland, you know. I mean, we were at war. We were-- so-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no plane going to France or to England. [LAUGHS] You know what I mean? It was impossible-- or Czechoslovakia. Austria was, was occupied by the Germans. And the Austrians, they were very happy about this because the Germans became greater or were greater anti-Semites than, than the Germans were. So she said, let me find out. And she called me two or three days later, I should come. And I-- I went there. And to make a long story short, she got me a, a plane ticket opened, which could be paid in German mark, with the Lufthansa, from Munich to Barcelona.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she said, I cannot give you any specific date. You will be in a standby. And you will have to go to the airport every day and see whether they take you. That's all. That's what I did. I went to Munich again. I went to the airport a few times. I was not-- I couldn't get on the plane. And then one day I, I was thinking, that doesn't work out. One day, I was just ready to go back again when they said, go ahead. I couldn't believe it. I had to open-- I had two suitcases with me. I mean, that's what I could take along. And they opened the suitcase. I remember that very distinctly. They took out-- a book was on top. I was reading a book which was-- I liked that book very much. It was how to get ahead in life, something like that, in German, you know. And the guy who, who opened the, the suitcases, well, he took it out for himself. And then I was running with my two suitcases, you know, across the field. The propellers already were, were moving. It was a two-propeller plane. There was a, a ladder. You had to  go up with a ladder. I took up one suitcase. And then I took up the other suitcase. The ladder was pulled up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I couldn't believe it. You know, I had never-- I'd never been close to an airplane in my life. I'd only seen airplanes in the air. And I, I just couldn't believe that I was-- I was sitting in an airplane. I mean, it was like a fairy tale, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I started looking around. And most of the people sitting in there were-- it wasn't even-- it wasn't even full. It was maybe half-full. And they were in uniform. They were German officers going to Spain. And there was one woman sitting there, an elderly woman. And I was sitting next to her. I was going to sit next to her. And after about 10 or 15 minutes, I felt terrible. I got airsick. And I was going to the-- to the bathrooms. Oh, I-- first I thought I was going to die. Then I, I, I hoped I would have died already. I felt just terrible. And I was still sitting there with my-- with my German-- with a raincoat, which was a very heavy plastic because you couldn't get anything else at that time in Germany. And everything you got, you got with rations, you know, with ration coupons. In fact, just to go back for one moment, when I left, there was only one Jewish store in town. We couldn't go on a plane anymore. We couldn't go on a-- on a streetcar anymore. We couldn't go in a barber shop anymore. We couldn't go-- we could only walk on the street, and that we couldn't do after 6 o'clock in the afternoon, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a curfew, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So you had to buy in that one-- in that one Jew store, what they called it. You had to walk there. You walked there for about-- depending where you lived, for about an hour, an hour and a half, and then back again. And usually when you got there with your coupons, they didn't have whatever you had the coupons for. So it was a terrible situation. But then the-- let me-- I got off--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on a tangent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. I wanted to ask you, you-- did you get a chance to say goodbye--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your family or grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I said goodbye to my aunt. I said goodbye to my grandmother. I remember my grandmother was [LAUGHS] putting potatoes on the, the fire there in the kitchen. And I said-- because she was crying-- [CRYING] Sorry. [CRYING] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wanted to be a little bit humorous, you know? [CRYING] And as I [? turned ?] that day, the potatoes boiled too long now. And I went to the-- to the railroad station in Stuttgart. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to walk there with the two suitcases. And by railroad, I went to, to Munich. I said goodbye to my aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was maybe not quite as bad because we felt that maybe I could go in a-- get on a plane, you know what I mean? So it was always that doubt in our mind. But still, for my grandmother, it was it. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did your family in Czechoslovakia know that you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I visited-- I visited my family in Czechoslovakia, which was in-- about half a year before I left. I got a-- an authorization by the Gestapo, and-- because I worked on the Auswandererstelle. Because you couldn't get that normally. And I got a, a, a permit to go on a train to visit my parents in Brunn. I had to go from Stuttgart to Vienna. There, I had to change trains, and then to Brunn, cross the border. And then, of course, we, we talked. And my brother was there. And my parents lived in one room. In one room they lived with my brother. And my mother had a little kitchen range there, just with two burners. That's where she cooked. And I was there for about one week. And then we said goodbye, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But at that time, my parents didn't know yet that I was going to leave Germany because it was six months before I went to Munich, you know. So it wasn't that bad when I say goodbye to my-- to my parents and to my brother. So let me go to, to Spain now, if I may. Yes.
